name: Babalu-Aye
description: Babalu-Aye, also known as Obaluaye, is a powerful and complex deity in Yoruba mythology, deeply revered in the Santería and Candomblé traditions. He is the orisha of infectious diseases, healing, and the earth. His name translates to 'Lord of the Earth' and is often associated with suffering, hardship, and the transformative power of illness. Babalu-Aye is seen as both a bringer and curer of diseases, holding the power to inflict plagues and epidemics but also possessing the knowledge and ability to heal and alleviate suffering. He is particularly associated with skin diseases, smallpox, and other ailments that leave visible marks. Despite his connection to illness, he is also a protector of the vulnerable and a source of hope for those afflicted. Offerings and prayers are made to him seeking healing, protection from disease, and guidance through difficult times. He is often depicted as an elderly man, sometimes covered in sores or wearing sackcloth, reflecting his association with poverty and suffering. His presence is a reminder of the fragility of life and the importance of compassion and healing. He is a complex figure, representing both the pain and the potential for renewal and transformation that can arise from adversity. He embodies the understanding that suffering can lead to growth and deeper appreciation for life.
subtitle: Orisha of Disease, Healing, and Earth

keyMyths.title: Babalu-Aye's Illness
keyMyths.description: Babalu-Aye was struck with a terrible illness as punishment for his arrogance and vanity. He learned humility and compassion through his own suffering.
keyMyths.source: Various Santería and Candomblé oral traditions

keyMyths.title: The Dog's Loyalty
keyMyths.description: Dogs are sacred to Babalu-Aye because they showed him loyalty and compassion when he was afflicted with disease and shunned by others. They licked his wounds and stayed by his side.
keyMyths.source: Various Santería and Candomblé oral traditions

keyMyths.title: Offerings and Healing
keyMyths.description: Offerings of popcorn, beans, and other simple foods are made to Babalu-Aye to appease him and seek his healing power. He is believed to respond to sincere devotion and compassion.
keyMyths.source: Various Santería and Candomblé oral traditions

sources.author: Migene González-Wippler
